text: This chapter synthesizes multiple lines of evidence to evaluate past, present, and future transformations within the global water cycle. It builds upon and complements the findings presented in Chapters 2, 3, and 4 regarding observed and projected hydrological changes, as well as the discussions in Chapters 10 and 11 focused on regional climate shifts and extreme events. Our assessment encompasses the fundamental physical mechanisms driving these changes, an analysis of observed trends and their primary drivers, future projections alongside their inherent uncertainties, and the risk of abrupt transitions. To provide a comprehensive view, we integrate paleoclimate records, modern observations, reanalyses, and both global and regional model simulations. The results reveal widespread and non-uniform human-induced alterations to the water cycle. While these changes were partially masked during the 20th century by competing climate drivers, they are poised to be increasingly dominated by greenhouse gas forcing on a global scale.
